attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with progressive weakness and numbness in both legs, developing over two hours. She completed two recreational scuba dives to 30 metres depth earlier today, surfacing from the last dive eight hours ago. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She is an experienced recreational diver.

On examination, she appears anxious. Her pulse rate is 100/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.0°C. She has reduced power (4/5) and altered sensation in both legs. The remainder of the neurological examination is normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Magnetic resonance imaging of the spine
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with neurological symptoms following scuba diving, consistent with decompression sickness. The gradual onset of bilateral neurological symptoms several hours after diving is characteristic. The depth and multiple dives increase the risk of decompression sickness due to greater nitrogen loading in tissues.

Hyperbaric oxygen therapy is the definitive treatment for decompression sickness and should be initiated as soon as possible. While arranging transfer to a hyperbaric facility, the patient should receive 100% oxygen via a non-rebreather mask. Recompression therapy reduces bubble size and helps eliminate inert gas while providing enhanced oxygenation to affected tissues.

Head CT scan would be appropriate if arterial gas embolism was suspected, which typically presents immediately or within minutes of surfacing with more focal neurological deficits. The delayed onset and symmetric presentation make this less likely.

Magnetic resonance imaging of the spine might eventually be performed to exclude other causes of myelopathy, but it should not delay definitive treatment with hyperbaric oxygen therapy when decompression sickness is clinically suspected.

Intravenous methylprednisolone is not indicated in the treatment of decompression sickness. While there may be an inflammatory component to the condition, steroids have not been shown to improve outcomes.

Observation alone would be inappropriate as neurological decompression sickness is a medical emergency requiring immediate intervention. Delay in treatment can lead to permanent neurological deficits.
learningPoints: Type II decompression sickness affecting the central nervous system requires immediate recompression therapy.
Neurological symptoms occurring within 24 hours of diving suggest decompression sickness rather than arterial gas embolism.
100% oxygen should be administered while arranging definitive hyperbaric treatment for decompression sickness.
references: Ortega MA, Fraile-Martinez O, García-Montero C, Callejón-Peláez E, Sáez MA, Álvarez-Mon MA, García-Honduvilla N, Monserrat J, Álvarez-Mon M, Bujan J, Canals ML. A General Overview on the Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy: Applications, Mechanisms and Translational Opportunities. Medicina (Kaunas). 2021 Aug 24;57(9):864. doi: 10.3390/medicina57090864. PMID: 34577787; PMCID: PMC8465921.

Cooper JS, Hanson KC. Decompression Sickness. [Updated 2023 Dec 13]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK537264/

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
